title: Denmark
dek: Nine days around one of Europe's most liveable cities — canal cruises and rooftop ski slopes, a bridge to Sweden, an overnight ferry to Norway, and three days at Pokémon GO Fest. Tick items off as you go; your progress saves automatically.
footer: Location photos via Wikimedia Commons — tap any credit for its source, author and license.
intro: Copenhagen base at CABINN by Nørreport, with day trips to Malmö and an overnight sailing to Oslo.
verified: ✓ Verified Jun 2026 — GO Fest tickets/add-ons/spawns/raids confirmed from pokemongo.com official pages + Leek Duck. Oslo bus tour (332 NOK, departs 10:25) confirmed via Go Nordic Cruiseline sightseeing page. Malmöhus Castle hours (Wed 11:00–17:00), Malmö Saluhall hours, and shopping guide confirmed from primary sources. City Pass, Copenhagen Card, ferry operator, Torvehallerne, and Folkehuset Absalon prices all confirmed. Re-check: Akershus interior price (≈150 NOK — conflicting sources), Malmöhus entry price (≈120 SEK — verify before going), Oslo bus tour availability/inclusion on your booking, Tivoli dynamic pricing, Hard Rock Cafe Oslo (confirm still open). · Citations recertified 17 Jul 2026 (post-trip): two cited sources have gone offline — the Go Nordic Cruiseline sightseeing page and grundtvigskirke.dk — so the Oslo tour price (≈332 NOK) and Grundtvig’s 15:45 close can no longer be checked at source; both are flagged in place with the conflicting figures other listings now give, rather than quietly restated. Turning Torso access corrected from “rare summer tours” to what Visit Skåne actually says (booked visits need a group of 10+; summer access only possible on confirmation). Verizon TravelPass re-confirmed at $12/line/day and its citation moved to the current support page.

learnings.summary: The anchors held; the walking didn’t. GO Fest and the Oslo cruise both delivered, and concentrating GO Fest into a single park was the best call of the trip. What bent the plan was mobility, not weather or fatigue — this was a five-person family trip, and a guide built for confident walkers met stairs at every train overpass, subway routes that were usable but far too walk-heavy, and a Malmö day that ended early because the distances were simply too much. Taxis were cheap, so Uber quietly became the default. The guide itself was the other weak point: thinner than Korea’s, with no daily forecast and none of Korea’s day-by-day scrolling itinerary.
keyLearnings: Plan for the slowest walker, not the average one. The subway was perfectly usable on paper and unusable in practice — the walking each leg demanded put it out of reach for this party, so Uber became the default. Taxis were affordable enough that this cost little.

Step-free matters more than proximity. The base area was pleasant but its train overpasses were reached by stairs, which cut off otherwise-navigable ground. A route that is short but has steps is not a short route for this group.

A summer booking without AC is a real problem — the hotel had none and the windows stayed open every night. This is the second trip in a row where missing AC was called out, so it belongs in the booking checklist as a hard filter, not an amenity to hope for.
One park beat a city-wide plan. Concentrating GO Fest into a single green space absorbed most of the days and worked well — no commuting between spawn clusters, everyone in one place.

Malmö did not justify its billing: a short day trip, not much to do once there, and walking distances beyond what the group could manage. A half-day cross-border hop needs a stronger reason than “it’s close”.

Oslo left almost no free time once the guided tour finished — enough for a pier walk, a Starbucks mug and a bakery before reboarding. Plan a cruise-day port stop backwards from the reboarding time, not forwards from arrival.
Prices were high across the board and the guide under-prepared the group for it. Budget figures need to be sharper and more honest, not indicative.
Distance to a convenience store shaped daily eating. IKEA and the nearby mall became the default meals — cheap, reliable, close.

Two guide features were missed by name: a daily weather forecast (the weather kept turning over and was hard to plan around) and Korea’s scrolling day-by-day itinerary, which would have made the days far easier to run.
changed: Recorded what actually happened on the days below, so the plan and the reality sit side by side rather than the guide implying it went to script.

Split docs/TRAVELER_PATTERNS.md by travel party. It described a single group — Korea’s three mid-20s friends — but Denmark was a five-person family trip with mobility constraints the Korea patterns say nothing about. Merging them would have handed the next guide advice drawn from the wrong travellers.

Promoted the durable lessons (step-free routing, plan-for-the-slowest-walker, AC as a hard filter, cheap-taxi-beats-transit, port-stop timing) into the traveler-patterns file the guide author consults, so the next guide starts from them.

days.actually: Cut short. Malmö turned out to have little the group wanted to do, and the distances on foot were more than the group could take — the half-day became less than that.

GO Fest worked, concentrated into a single park rather than spread across the city. ⚠ The account names King’s Garden (Kongens Have); the plan and the official venue were Fælledparken — recorded as reported, unreconciled.
Much the same as Friday — most of the day spent in the one park rather than running the citywide stamp-and-spawn loop the plan laid out.
Barely any time ashore once the guided bus tour was done: a walk along a nearby pier, a Starbucks mug, a bakery, then back aboard. The free afternoon the plan promised did not really exist.
